channel.title: Bull & Bear With Me
channel.description: Markets, explained without the finance-bro energy. A daily 4-minute read for regular people.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 14, 2026
item.description:
# Bull & Bear With Me ### Wednesday, April 15, 2026
---
**📈 The Open**
Markets are basically back to all-time highs, Goldman had one of its best quarters ever, and somehow both of those were apparently bad news — welcome to earnings season, where nothing makes sense and the points are made up.
---
**🎯 Big Three**
• **The Nasdaq just went ten for ten.** Ten straight days of gains — that's the longest winning streak since 2021. The S&P is now sitting less than 1% from its all-time high, which means the market has almost fully shaken off the whole "Middle East war / oil shock" thing from a few weeks ago. Resilient as hell, or completely delusional. Possibly both.
• **Goldman Sachs had its second-best quarter in company history and the stock still dropped.** Near-record profits. Shares down. This is earnings season in a nutshell — Wall Street doesn't care what you *did*, only what you're *going to do*. It's like going 14-2 in the regular season and your fantasy league still talks shit because your playoff schedule looked tough. JPMorgan beat estimates too, and *also* fell. Wells Fargo just straight-up missed and got dunked on. Banks are having a weird week.
• **US-Iran tensions are the main event right now.** The US has a naval blockade on Iranian oil in the Strait of Hormuz — a narrow stretch of water where something like 20% of the world's oil passes through. Iran apparently reached out to Washington, and talk of negotiations sent oil prices pulling back. Oil is sitting around $92 a barrel. If that number goes up, everything gets more expensive — gas, groceries, shipping. The IMF (a big global economic organization, basically the world's financial referee) is warning this could tip us toward a global recession. Not there yet. But it's the thing to watch.
---
**🧠 One Thing Worth Understanding**
**Why do bank stocks fall when earnings are good?**
Here's the thing nobody explains: a stock price isn't about what happened — it's about what people *expected* to happen. If everyone already figured Goldman was gonna crush it, that expectation gets baked into the stock price *before* earnings drop. Then the report comes out, it's great, and there's nothing left to get excited about. Traders call it "buy the rumor, sell the news." It's like hyping a movie for two years — by opening night, you've already kind of seen it. Goldman had a monster quarter and the stock still slipped because the bar was already sky-high. Doesn't mean the company is struggling. It just means Wall Street already priced in the win.
---
**👀 Keep An Eye On**
- **More bank earnings rolling in** — Bank of America and Citigroup report tomorrow. After the JPMorgan and Wells Fargo whipsaw, set your expectations accordingly. - **Any update on US-Iran talks** — A second round of negotiations could push oil lower and markets higher. Things fall apart and it goes the other direction fast.
---
**☕ The Close**
CarMax dropped 15% yesterday. Used car prices are finally coming down, which is great for everyone buying a car and apparently catastrophic for everyone selling them. We contain multitudes.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 13, 2026
item.description:
**📈 The Open**
The U.S. just blockaded the Strait of Hormuz, oil hit $104 a barrel, inflation jumped to 3.3%, Goldman missed on bonds, and somehow the S&P is basically flat. Markets are fine. Everything is fine.
---
**🎯 Big Three**
- **The Strait of Hormuz is blockaded.** About 20% of the world's oil flows through that narrow waterway, and we just put a Navy cork in it. Gas was already $4.12 a gallon before this. Analysts are calling it the worst energy disruption since the 1970s, which is a hell of a thing to read before your Monday morning coffee.
- **Inflation jumped from 2.4% to 3.3% in one month.** That's not a drift. That's a lurch. The Fed has two jobs — keep inflation down and keep employment up — and right now both are quietly going sideways. Their response, per Vice Chair Jefferson: our policy is "well-positioned." Comforting stuff, truly.
- **Goldman Sachs had a near-record quarter and still dropped 4%.** Revenues up 14%. EPS beat. But the bond desk missed by $850 million and Wall Street punished them like they showed up to the company picnic without potato salad. The rest of the big banks — JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Citi, BofA — report this week. Could be the most expensive week of earnings season to actually pay attention to.
---
**🧠 One Thing Worth Understanding**
**The Strait of Hormuz, and why it matters to your drive to work.**
The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow strip of water between Iran and Oman — about 21 miles wide at its tightest point. Something like 20-21% of all the world's oil passes through it every single day, tankers carrying crude from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE to the rest of the planet. When that flow gets disrupted — whether by conflict, blockade, or threats — oil prices spike globally because traders don't wait around to see how it plays out. They panic-buy futures and the price jumps immediately, which is why WTI crude went up 8% in one morning. That feeds into gas prices within days, then into shipping costs, then into basically everything that gets transported or made with energy, which is everything. The 1970s oil crisis caused recessions across the Western world. We're not there yet. But the direction of travel is not great, Bob.
---
**👀 Keep An Eye On**
- **Bank earnings all week.** JPMorgan reports Tuesday and tends to set the tone. If they miss, expect the "soft landing" crowd to go quiet for a few days. - **Oil prices Monday night and Tuesday.** Any further escalation around the blockade could push WTI toward $110. That's the number that really starts showing up in everything.
---
**☕ The Close**
Markets are "pricing in the geopolitical risk," which is analyst-speak for "we have no idea what happens next, but we've agreed to be calm about it." Respect the process.
